CURRENT AFFAIBS,

The I.A.0.S. has succeeded in keeping its hand in
the public purse. As we read it, the Society that runs
the gloomy Celtic Christmas is to get a pro rala grant,
and by that we understand that the Department will
dole out a shilling for every shilling subscribed by the
members. The Council of Agriculture, at its previous
meeting, resolved that the Dopartment should promote
agricultural organisation, and provide the funds for
that work. Having got that resolution passed at the
previous meeting, more than half the battle wag won
for the organisation that annually blossoms into : opal
hushes ”’ and other things in an annual most uqsmtwblc
for farmers, not to talk of other classes of scn§1.ble and
normal human beings. That resgoluhon thaving been
got through, the practical question was—whether to
attempt to organise through Mr. Anderson’s Society,
through the County Councils, or through a new Organi-
sation, The resolution placed Mr'..Anderson s sazlary
in a rather secure position. Organising through thirty-
two 'County Councils does not strike one as very prac-
ticable, and why start a new Organisation when one

—beo its success, or want of it, what it may—already

oxists? It soems to us that those who opposed the
grant to Mr. Anderson’s Society should have fought
out the matter at the previous Council meeting, and
have refused to commit the Department to the work of
promoting agricultural organisation. Once the Council
committed .itself to that, Mr. Anderson’s Society had
rather the better part of the argument. The main
reply to the Society of which our “opal-hush” friend,
the Celtic Christmas, is the official annual, wasthat as it
boasted of its great extent and success, it was reason-
able to allow those who benefited by it to pay for its
upkeep. Z

We fear that the decision to subsidise the I.A.O.S.
from public funds will not lead to greater harmony in
the country. The great fundamental question remains
—why should those not in the I.A.O.S., or those who
rightly or wrongly disagree with the methods and pro-
cedure of the I.A.O.S., be compelled to pay for its
upkeep ?

Sir Horace Plunkett suffers, perhaps not unnaturally,
from Department on the brain. In the course of his
address, as reported, to the Council, he said :—“Tt is
only six years since this new branch of government was
set up, with the primary purpose of placing the chief
wealth-producers in Ireland on an equality with their
compelitors abroad, and in these six years we have had
opportunities of studying the causes of the disadvan-
tages our farmers are under.”’

The lines we have italicised are clearly symptoms of
the malady from which Sir Horace suffers. We are
not unappreciative of what the new Department could
do for Ireland; we did a lot, so far as our influence
went, to make it popular, and to induce the people to
use it for all it was worth, even though we also felt it
our duty to rap it on the knuckles now and then. But
ab its best it could only be a “department,”’ and Sir
Horace only talked raimeis when he spoke of it as an
instrument for placing “the chief wealth-producers of
Ireland on an equality with their competitors abroad.”
There are other things besides co-operation, subsidised
“opal hushes’’ and Departmental leaflets in that
equation, Horatio! If the Irish farmers are to be
brought into line with those of other countries. the job
in hand is considerably greater than any mere Depart-
ment work—there is the over-taxation question, the
national government, or rather want of national govern-
ment, question, and various other things. It is a pity
that Sir Horace should add confusion to an already com-
plex situation by raimeis that is a symptom of Depart-
ment on the brain.

There was a reference in his speech which, we think,
deserves severe condemnation. As our readers know,
we are no partisans of a public character known to some
as “ Honest John,”” and to others as a “ Melancholy
Humbug.””  Indeed, we have a particular charge
against him that, though he was a large shareholder
in the Great Northern Railway, he never raised even his
voice on the floor of the Railway Board Room to pro-
test against the state of things disclosed by us relating
to the staffing of that Company. In view of our atti-
tude towards Mr, Dillon, we are all the more impartial
in protesting against a civil servant’s attack upon him.
Sir Horace is not in Parliament ; and thinks, now, that
one in his position ought not to be in Parliament.
Therefore we must take him as an ordinary paid official,
an individual in the Civil Service. Sir Horace said:—
“If the charge implies that the I.A.O.S., nominally
economic and social in its aims, is really political, and

{chat the design is so deep that it can only be seen
through \by the higher intelligence of the trader of the
town, then the Irish farmer is such a fool that he had
better leave his Boeotia and go and brighten his intelli-
gence in Ballaghadereen or some other contre of eco-
nomic thought.” This reference to Mr, Dillon may be
witty, it may be deserved, but our point is that it is
not playing the game. If Sir Horace elects to stick on
for bare life to his job, whatever Government may be
in or out, he ought to accept the limitations of an
official in a good job, and leave party politics alone. If
Sir Horace made an attack on Mr. Moore, M.P:, or
Mr. Long, M.P., never fear some of England’s Faith-
ful Garrison on the Council of Agriculture would bring
him promptly to book.

In the course of a letter, Mr. A. S. Lough, one of the
most prominent co-operators in the country, said:—

If tho Council clearly realises what the I.A.0.S. now is—namely,
a committee elected and controlled by a number of trade organi-
sations whose reason for existenco is to get higher prices for what
they sell and pay lower prices for what they buy by using tho
co-operative principle instead of individual trading—it will be
seen how awkward and unfair it is for officials of a Government
Department fo sot up this particular trading committeo and supply
funds for its use. Again, the I.A.O.S. subsidises a weekly news-
paper, which would be dead but for this help. This paper is
unscrupulous in its atiacks on members of the Council and others
who criticise its patron. Surely the Department should not be
even indirectly responsible for such a journal as the ‘ Irish Home-
stead.” It has been proved and admitted that the LA.O.S. are
canvassers for the wholesale trading federations.

The weak point about all the adverse criticism of
the I.A.0O.8. was that the Council had already decided
that the Department should promote co-operation, so
that the real question was rather—is subsidising the
Society, whatever be its weaknesses and sins, a lesser
evil than attempting to carry out the previous resolu-
tion of the Council by some other method?

Mr. McKenna, who opposed any subsidies to Mr.
Anderson’s Society, very sensibly remarked:— He
wished every organisation, agricultural or otherwise,
got up for the benefit of the people all success, but if
they were started as self-help movements, let them
stand by themselves and prove to their members that
they were worthy of support.”” And Father McLoug-
lain, of County Leitrim, remarked :— They had told
them that they were representing flourishing Socicties,
but the fun of the thing was that the farmers appeared
to know nothing about this mysterious thing—the
Organisation—which was something out of the clouds
(laughter).” “Out of the clouds” was at least an
appropriate phrase to use concerning a Society that
breathes an annual “opal hush,’”” and yawns a yearly
*“ grey twilight.””

Mr. D. J. Cogan, M.P., remarked : —“ If the Society
had been doing work so successfully, it ought to be a
self-supporting body.” Mr. P. J. O’Neill, the Chair-
man of the Dublin County Council, spoke and voted
against placing the Society on the public funds. “The
question before the Council,”” said he, “was a question
of principle.”” We might perhaps add that it was also
a question of several gentlemon’s fat salaries, and
human nature being what it is, men often fight des-
perately for their jobs. The motion to subsidise the
the I.A.0O.8. was carried by 52 to 26, and so the staff
of the Celtic Clristmas may safely order an extra opal
zgrkey and another grey goose for their Christmas

inner.

with whom we trust none of our readers will confuso

was in the chair. The Chairman referred to the rela:
tions between Mr. B. and the polis. The following is

Mr. Gethings, present Emperor of Ireland, is not
madly in love with the Liberal Party in the Imperial
Parliament, oven though Mr. Clancy, Mr. Pot
O’Brien, and Mr. Tim-total Harrington are more or
less allies of it. The Chairman said:—

The uncertaintics of Parliament gave a prominenco to the Sun-
day Closing and Early Saturday Closing Bill which carly in the
cession oven the most skilled in Parliamentary procedure did not
beliove it could possibly occupy. The sccond reading was passed
by a large majority—as indeed any measure of the kind would be
in tho House of Commons as it was at present constituted (hear,
hear)—and it was roferred to one of the Standing Committecs.

It will be noted that the assembled Bungs said, ¢ heal.',
hear!” Some Bungs no doubt are anxious in their
minds as to whether Home Rule would increase the
takings and power of “the trade.”

The light that Mr. Gethings, Chairman of Bungs’
Association, threw on the compromise between Bung
and some of the Temperance people is interesting—it
takes a good deal of the gilt off Bung. He said:—

By means of their exertions they were, however, able to show
that in Ireland there was o wide difference of opinion as to what
would likely bo the results following the imposing of such restric-
tions as were embraced in this Bill. This circumstance might be
said to have been the foundation of the compromise which was
arrived at, and into the details of which it was not necessary for
him to enter. He trusted, however, they would accept his per-
sonal assurance that those immediately responsible for the pro-
tection of their interests did not enter into the negotiations which
took place without having carefully considered the situation or
without having consulted those who had always been their iriends
in Parliament. They had made a concession. They had made
what had been described as a very generous concession, and
he was sure as a body they would be very pleased indeed if good
results followed their action.

In plain words, Bung got a bit of a start, so he put
the best face he could on it and agreed to compromise.

The patriotic Bungs, according to Mr. Gethings, are
not opposed to the “minions’’ of the British Law in
Ireland. He said:—

As u body, they had no quarrel with the police; it was to being
harassed that they objected, and they belioved that they had made
it abundantly clear that for some years past they had not been

treated with that fair play to which, as citizens and ratepayers,
they believed they were cntitled.

Poor persecuted Bung!

Thero is evidently a bit of a “split’’ in “the trade,”
for a Mr. Wickham sounded a jarring note.  Bub
then Wickham’s “establishment >’ is, we understand,

We think the Irish Party ought at onco make a
Party question of bunging up Bung on St. Patrick’s
Day, and so bring the Pot O’Briens and Tim-totals to
heel. The Party can do very little in Parliament, and
that fact is being used against thom at presont, though
at present they are in a position to do some useful
work. One thing they should do is get the Bill
through for the closing of the public-houses on La
Feite Piopaix.  Irish Ireland has attempted the
work, but it has not succeeded. Bung in Dublin has
!Jeeu able to defy Irish Ireland, and humiliating though
it be, we have to admit that if Bung is to be closed up,
it can only be done by a Bill of the British Parliament.

The Party were able to give effective assistance in
the fight for the competitive principle in the clerical
staffing of the Great Northern Railway, and in their
own interests it would be well for them to use every
ounce of the very little power that they have in the
British Parliament.

By the way, Mr. Jeremiah MacVeagh, who took
such an active and effective part in the Great Northern
Railway affair, has contributed an article on the
struggle to the Glasgow Observer. In the course of that
article, Mr. MacVeagh wrote :—

A Railway is a Monopoly, conferred and guaranteed by Parlia-
ment on well-defined principles. The promoters cannot move a
step, cannot acquire a rood of land, cannot start a train, without
the sanction of Parliament. Their monopoly is a trust for the
benefit of the people; and if they abuse that trust Parliament is
quite entitled to refuse to confer further powers upon them. That
being so, the Irish members felt that the conduct of the Great
Northern Railway of Ireland had been so flagrant that they would
be justified in asking the House of Commons to assert its supre-

macy, and to insist that there should not henceforth be any
*“religious test” in the selection of its employees. The case was
overwhelming, and practically no defenco was attempted.  The

Dublin LEADER, in the columns of which the figures were first
published, and to which tho credit is due for forcing tho subject
under public notice, had established the case against the company
in the most conclusive manner.

The Free Library question is very much discussed
just now. Recently, a proposal that the Newcastle
West District Council should adopt the Public Libraries
Act of 1885 was defeated by 23 votes to 10, and on
the occasion there was a vigorous debate, in which
Monsignor Hallinan took a prominent part. A Free
Library or not a Free Library, is a practical question
to be decided upon its merits by each community for
whom it may be proposed to establish one. Although
Monsignor Hallinan is an enthusiastic Gaelic Leaguer,
although he did one man’s work in the agitation for
Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play on the Great Sourface
Railway, although he is a man of mature years and
mature judgment, we still venture to assert that on a
question such as that of establishing or not establish-
ing a Free Library for Newcastle West, he is at least:
as much entitled to a free expression of his views and
a free use of his legitimate influence as is, say, the
youngest of any of our present undergrowbh of infallible
philosophers. We are not convinced that the ten who
voted for the library necessarily possess all the wisdom,

" and that each one of the majority was necessarily a

tame dog whipped to heel by the domineering cleric.
These are not popular views in some wise quarters
where the prevailing attitude seems to be—when in

doubt go against the clergy.
Tho gentleman who proposed that the library be

founded, made a very sensible remark that really has
a groat bearing on discussions in all parts of Ireland.

THE LEADER. : 243

He said:—*They all knew it had been their (the
British Government’s) aim for the past hundred years
or more to keep them ignorant, and they had fairly

well succeeded (laughter).”” Quite so; and that is the
reason why the youngest amongst us should count ten
bofore he solemnly decided that his elders who, at
least, have experience of life, are necessarily altogether
fools. ~ We admit it, and the country would do well
to bear it in mind, that we are, in the main, an ignor-
ant as well as a green people; under these circum-
stances it would be wellif even the preparatory-grade
pass boy of “advanced’’ views would admit, even if
only for the sake of argument, that men of forty or fifty
or sixty, even though they might be of the “surpliced
ruffians of Maynooth,”” may possibly have a little bit
of reason and sense on their side when one of them rises
“to make a few remarks.”’

We have read Monsignor Hallinan’s speech. We
believe, with reservations, in Public Libraries, but we,
nevertheless, appreciate many grave points in Monsig-
nor Hallinan’s views. We cannot follow Monsignor
Hallinan when he says that as we have fought for
denominational education in our schools, that we must,
in the ‘sense that libraries are a continuation of our
education, logically fight for denominational libraries.
One of the hard facts of life is that many of us when
we come to face the world have to mix with Tom, Dick
and Harry, Turk, Jew, or Atheist, we go to England,
to Australia, to America. Denominational libraries
for young people and for middle-aged people of the
ignorant order have much to be said for them, but the
hard facts of life have to be faced. The young lads
from the country who win a oclerkship in the Great
Sourface Railway and come to Dublin, are liable, as a
result of reading the advertisements and puffs in the
“Truly Catholic’’ Freeman, to go to dirty plays. Yet
one would scarcely take that temptation out of the
way of country youths by advising country youths not
to take service of any kind in Dublin. The temptations
in the way of the unfortunate youths who go to
America are far worse.

Monsignor Hallinan referred to practical and
financial difficulties; these were very proper matters to
discuss, but, of course, they have a purely local interest.
The preponderating proportion of fiction that is read in
existing Public Libraries, and to which Monsignor Halli-
nan referred, knocks a good deal of the “romancing’>
out of the arguments of those who, in their ardour,
would almost have us believe that Public Libraries are
a panacea for all evils, including the old-crusted evil
of emigration. Some books classed as “fiction’’ are
beyond price and praise; but if 90 per cent. of books
read in a Public Library are fiction. the ratepayers may
well say—if some giddy people want that sort of thing,
let them buy it with their own money. On the other
hand, the Public Library need only stock a certain
amount of books of fiction, and only books that have
an established reputation.

A new but rather formidable argument raised

*against Free Libraries by Monsignor Hallinan, is an

argument from the Irish-Ireland point of view. He
said :—

During tho past 6 years I have, by word and oxample, by my
pen and by my purse, done ono man's work on behalf of the Irish
language revival movement. I bogan to learn—and am still
learning--tho Irish language. I was tho first who got permission
from the National Board to employ extern teachers in the schools
of non-Trish-speaking ' districts. Since then the Irish has been
taught in all tho schools of this parish. Though during my whole
lifo I was never a member of any political league, I becamo Pro-
sident of the Gaelic Loague, and still continue to be so in my own
parish. Why do I montion theso porsonal matters? I do so to
ostablish my claim to speak about the Irish language and Irish-
Ireland movement. That movement has, thank God, made, and is
still making, steady progress, and I hope it will continue to do
s0. But in my humblo opinion no greater calamity could befall
it than to have it in any way idontified with the Free Library
movement. The ono is the antithesis of the other. The true Irish-
Trolander wants to keop before the Irish people the grand ideals

i

%

of his forefathers. The Free Library man puts before them English
ideals of the smart society set through tho pages of the English
novel. The true Irish-Irelander wants to preserve the customs of
our forefathers, their singing and dancing and pastimes, in thoir
own homes and those of their neighbours; but tho Freo Library
man wants the boys and girls to leave their homes at night and
crowd into the free building near the publichouso in the neigh-
bouring village, and thero spend their time in poring over tho
latest works of English fiction, or some such kindred works. And
this is how the process of Anglicisation is to bo arrested! And
this is how the Irish Nation is to bo built up! If tho Frco
Libraries are introduced into the building, the dry rot will soon
set in, and tho rising generation that aro now loarning tho Irish
language, and singing and dancing in the schools under proper
supervision of their clergy and teachers, and in their own homes
under the eyes of their parents, will become a generation of novel-
readers, taking their ideals from across the Irish Sca. And thus
th_e Free Library Irish Irelanders, cither knowingly or unwittingly,
will have become the most active and poworful agencies for tho
more rapid Anglicisation of the Irish people.

As we have said, this is a new argument, and we
think it was well that it has been pushed forward.
Of course the conduct of Public Libraries could be so
arranged that this Anglicising tendency could be re-
duced; but as against that, it would be likely enough
that a majority of the Committee would be Anglicised
and would regard Irish-Ireland as obscurant, re-action-
ary and a fad.

The question was discussed again one night last week
at the Calaroga Literary and Debating Club, Dublin.
The auditor of the club, Mr. T. P. Fox, made “ Public
Libraries ’’ the subject of his inaugural address. We
do not share Mr. Fox’s hopes in the efficacy of Public
Libraries in restraining the peasantry from emigrating.
Emigration is, unfortunately, the effect of causes other
than the lack of Public Libraries. Mr. Lyster rose
to the height of a prose-poem in his oration in favour
of Public Libraries; but, then, he is a librarian, and
like the rest of us, cannot be blamed for growing en-
thusiastic on—shop. Father George O’Neill, S.J., took
some of the shine off the polish that other speakers had
raised on the Libraries. We do not quite follow him,
however, when he said :—* They should see that their
educational house was in order before they devoted
themselves to the work of adding a greenhouse. While
their University question was so unsettled they would
be very much in the position of persons anxious about
their greenhouse while their dwellinghouse wanted a
roof.””  Might not one as well set about building a
greenhouse, if the alternative is mere idleness owing
to being prevented by superior force from working on
the roof of the house? The Chairman of the meeting,
Father A. Coleman, the distinguished Dominican
author, pithily remarked that “ one thing in favour of
public libraries was the fact that it .would help
to do away with private libraries which provided all
kinds of poisonous literature.” By the way, however
trashy the fiction of a Public Library in Ireland might
be, it would probably be, on the whole, some degrees
above the average quality of the fare offered for sale in
the ordinary newsagents’ shops and stalls. But again,
that would not necessarily be a compelling reason why
it should be supplied out of the rates. Truly, as
Father O’Neill remarked, it was “a difficult, complex,
and, at the same time, most practical question.”’

‘We take the following from the Glasgow Weckly
Mail:—“One writer, referring to Orangeism, remarked
that it was a form of insanity peculiar to the North of
Ireland. That writer has never, surely, been in Scot-
land on the Twelfth, or he would have discovered that
the Orange germs taken from the North of Ireland and
transplanted in Scdtland are very productive.””

‘What is the Press doing for the Anti-Treating League
Campaign in the West? There is a Temporance cru-
sade going on in parts of Connacht, but the dailly
papers tell us little or nothing of it. ‘We suppose the
ha’penny Independent people are too busy hunting up
divorce cases, and looking after the intereste of Hum-

- phrey’s Dump, and the Simply Deplorable is probably

DECEMBER 8, 1906.

so agitated about its own affairs that it is in no humour
to do anything that might put up Mr. Bung’s back.
What of the Dark Crusaders \themselves, if we may
refer to these excellent missionaries in that free and
casy way. The Crusade that was begun in the Clon-
fert diocese a year ago, was renewed in October and
November with great success, yet we do not recollect
seeing oven a reference to it in the ha’penny organ of
Humphrey’s Dump and divorce cases, or in Matty Bod-
kin’s Budget. Of course the Temperance Crusaders,
unlike the “Spring Chicken ’’ and *Zaza " people, do
not advertise! - We understand that the Missioners
in the Clonfert diocese found that the Anti-Treating
Leaguo was not only a success, bub a great success.
The Capuchins, the Vincentians, and the Redemp-
torists have been crusading. ~Why do not some of
them, as the Dublin dailies don’t do the work, do the
work for the Dublin dailies, and so let the people in
other parts know what is being done in the West?

The following advertisement is clipped from a Scotch
paper of December Ist:—“Washhouse forewoman
wanted for steam laundry in Dublin; must thoroughly
understand the business; applications will be treated
confidentially—Address 505, Citizen Office.”” What!
Is Ireland not able to produce even a washerwoman!?
What has “ Honi Soit ** from Dartry, the Chairman of
the very exclusive Rathmines Commissioners, been doing
if there are not a few trained Irish washerwomen in
the labour market by now. Perhaps the School of

WHY BE AN EMPIRE ?

HE discussion on  Sinn  pein—by the way, how
many people can pronounce the first of those two
words correctly—in this paper, has raised the broader
question of Imperialism, as a whole. How far is the Im-
perial spirit, not as confined to the British Empire, but
as embodied in every Empire, wherever it exist, a spirit
worthy of approval, or at all events capable of justifi-
cation? Does it tend to the ennoblement of the human
being, to the progress of art, science and civilisation?
Now no word is used in a looser sense than Imperialism,
and before entering on the discussion of its merits, it
may be well to inquire what in very truth an “Empire’’
means. If we do so, we must see that a very remarkable
change has taken place in its signification within quite
recent times. Down to the beginning of the nine-
teenth century, the essential idea of “ an Empire ’’ was
that the people who made up “the Empire’’ proper
were not free. They might be equal, or almost equal,
under a despot as in the Roman Empire, especially in
later days, when a common servitude was the lot of all.
Or else they might—and this has ever been the harshest
form of Empire—be the vassals of a despot people, itself
enjoying free institutions. This was the state of things
England sought to establish between itself and the
American Colonies at the time of the war of Indepen-
dence. There were, of course, also the merely nominal
Empires of the Middle Ages. But one idea, that of
supremacy, whether of a Government or of a people,
over the rest, was the feature common to all.

To-day, we find the word Empire used in a very
different sense. In the German Empire, for instance,
the Prussian enjoys, so far as the constitution goes, no
supremacy over the citizen of Bavaria or Wurtemberg.
The term “Empire’’ is used to denote a combination
upon equal terms, having its origin in L_he consent of
tho several parties, much as in the constitution of the
United States. German Poland and Elsass-Lothringen
are the oppressed exceptions. In Austria-Hungary the
case is even more remarkable. There wo find 2 num-
ber of different nations speaking different tongues, .
formed into the union of an Empire-Kingdom upon
fairly equal terms. Perfect justice is. nob indeed al\vays
attained, but the Austrian Poles, differing from their
German brethren ,are quite contented, and the Czechs

248

and the Croats have been conceeded important national
Tights. *Empire in these cases means confederacy, and,
Like the citizens of Virginia and of Texas, the soveral
constituent nations have no desire to terminate the
union. Thero is a single Imperial Government combined
with distinct local Governments, and ‘with this arrange-
mont sveryone is fairly satisfied. If thero is not much
Lencfit from the combination, there is at any rate no
apparent harm.

There is, however, a third system, and though it is
perhaps not quite as bad as the ruthless selfishness of
the early conception of an Empire founded on supre-
macy, it may have results equally disastrous. This
is the system, which France adopts. It is to take
several nations, for instance, the French, the Algerians
and the Hindus of Pondicherry and treat them as one
nation. It is in accordance with this view, that the
Ainhabitants of Algiers and Pondicherry send xepre-
sentatives to the French Parliament. But such a
system being quite impossible of application in practice,
usually results, in the working out, in establishing
government by bureaucracy of the least desirable kind.

Now if we come to analyse that which most of us are
thinking of all the time, namely, the British Empire,
we shall find it a strange conglomeration of all these
opposed systems. In the Government of India and
Egypt, we have the rankest despotism, that hearkens to
no voice of the governed. The Government of Repub-
lican Rome was not more ruthless in its contempt for
the provincial. On the other hand, we see the relation
between the United Kingdom and the great self-govern-
ing Colonies, where the tie is looser than that in any
other Empire whatsoev Everything of independence
save the conduct of foreign affairs is conceded to these
dependencies; though they are in theory subject to the
central Parliament.  Many a small nation, nominally
independent, enjoys no greater freedom of action than
they do. :
Next take the case of Ireland. Where does it come
in? Shall it be compared to India or to Canada?

TO READ, OR NOT TO READ?

THER;E has been a certain stirring of the waters
I lately on the subject of reading, and a somewhat
brisk current of discussion has rolled around the ques-
tion of }E‘ree Libraries. A debate on that topic gave
the coming season of the Calaroga Literary Society an
interesting send-off. The discussion, too, was genuine,
not one.of those heartless fakes that usually do duty
for a debate at the average inaugural address of tho
vew auditor of one of our Dublin debating societics.
The Auditor, Mr. Fox, took  Public Librarics " as his
subject, and read an earnest, well-built paper on it,
and in the discussion that followed, Count Plunkett,
Mr, T. W. Lyster, the Rev. George O’Neill, S.J., and
Alderman Thomas Kelly, spoke with varying degrees
of interest. The Auditor’s paper, and almost the
whole weight of the subsequent discussion, went full in
favour of the Free Public Libraries movement. In-
deed, had not the Rev. George O’Neill, S.J., taken the
part of advocatus diaboli, as he himself humorously put
it, all the speaking would have been an unqualified
support of the Auditor’s address. As the question of
Ifrcc Public Libraries is a live one at present, it may be
timely to say something about it. In doing so I shall,
of course, advert occasionally to the interesting debate
at the Calaroga Club.

Men cannot think without language, and languags
cannot fully develop without writing. Writing pos-
tulates reading, and reading postulates a different stage
of mental development between those who read and
those who don’t. Reading is now essential to study,
and study creates differences which there is no getting
over between those who pursue it and those who don’t.
Had it not been for books and libraries, there would
never have been a Leaper. This review sprang out of
the reading of something other than the Freeman, or
even the /ndependent. And the LEADER has made a
difference in some things in this country; some of the
newly-berthed clerks on the Irish railways ought to
know that. The Gaelic League sprang out of the
educated enthusiasm of educated men; books and libra-
ries had more to do with its birth than anything else,
except, no doubt, the inherited national impulses of its
founders. Books, largely, brought the Young Ireland
Party into being, and the literature which Davis and
his friends wrote owed its origin not merely to the
enthusiastic minds of its authors, but in large part
to the culture those minds had received.  The most
thoughtful of the leaders of the Fenians, of the Land
League, of the present National Party, have all been
men who had received an education, or given themselves
one by the aid of books. Indeed, without the results
and influences of books, it is hard to tell how Ireland
would stand to-day.

But perhaps all this is granted; certainly most of
the speakers at the Calaroga Club would have granted
it joyfully, and probably even the advocatus diaboli
himself—subject, of course, to proper “diabolic ”’ quali-
fications and reservations. What, then, is the diffi-
culty? Chiefly this: that some good Irishmen, in other
parts of Ireland, regard Free Libraries as a danger, or
a possible source of danger, to faith and morals. Leb
us grant that there is, possibly, some danger. How
then? Shall our people remain without the resources
of culture? Is there no danger there? I think there
is, and perhaps the greater danger. At least, I offer
that opinion, for in this matter we can only offer
opinions; the matter cannot be tested by any touch-
stone which will evoke scientifically accurate results.
Mr. Fox very rightly pointed out that if Free Libraries
be a danger, then the invention of printing must have
been a danger, and the faculty of reading a danger also.

THE IRISH MUSIC QUESTION.

S there is a long article by me on another subject
A in this week’s LEADER, I cannot conveniently say
much in reply to Father Bewerunge now. He appears
to grant what I haye been contending for, and what he
had formerly, as I understood him, denied; that is,
that the printed notation of Irish music is Irish music.
There is no good in saying that we are “thrown back
on tradition”” in the cases I instanced, namely, those
in which the traditional ”’ version is extinct. Petrio
and the rest may—must—have taken down their tunes

DEeCEMBER 8, 1906.

from country singers and players; bub in the cases
where tunes so derived are now (_axbmcb in the tra-
ditional ”” version, the printed music becomes the prime
authority, and fount of tradition. 3

There was, surely, no need to say that printed nota-
tion is not a melody. Wo all know that. Printed
music, even with the amplest expression marks and
other directions, postulates a living, intelligent
musician, just as much as a printed poem postulates
readers of that poem. Nevertheless, without the
printed music the musician i_s_ nowhere; simply no-
whore. Yes, but he has tradition to fall back upon?
Not in those cases where the traditional version is ex-
tinct. An expert musician can read music Mﬁnmlg,
without needing to perform it at all; your “tradi-
tional *’ musician cannot do that. .The expert can
transmit musical ideas to other musicians on paper, a
thing which the “ traditional > man c:ffmob do.

Concerning the question of “style, I hope to say
something at a later date. Father'Bo‘werunge Tepre-
sents mo as holding that Irish music ‘ ml})sb have an
intonation somewhat like the piano scale.”” Well, if
Trish music wants to live, I should think it must be
written in scales reproducible on the modern piano.
All that which the reverend writer says about
the Irish language is pointless; there is no analogy.
The “traditional '’ music was never written down by
its authors; the Irish poets could, and did, write down
their lyrics. One of these poets when dying wrote tha@,
“The poet is weak indeed when the pen falls from his
hand.” Not in these words, of course, but in Irish.

One word more. My critic, in quoting a passage
from me, puts the word “sic,”” and a note of admira-
tion, after ““adopts them,”’—in reference to the “vir-
tues”” of traditional music. - 'What is wrong with my
phrase? Shakespeare makes Hamlet say: ¢ Assume a
virtue, if you have it not.”” If we may assume a vir-
tue, why may we not adopt one? I have been wont
to think that I know how to write English—and I
think so still. Inraar.

THE RING SECONDARY SCHOOL.

Y article on the above school has served at least
one good purpose: it has brought to light some
important facts concerning the administration and
working of the school. I do not propose to deal with
all the points in the replies which my article has called
forth—many of them have no bearing on the question
at issue; I shall content myself with a review of the
statements I made, and the answers that have been
given in reply to them.

First, as regards Mr. Kiely’s qualifications for the
position of principal of a Secondary or Intermediate
School, I am informed that the only qualification he
can lay claim to is that of a Second-class National
School Teacher. I take his word for it that he passed
the examination for this grade of teacher with “Special
Distinction.” T respectfully submit that this qualifi-
cation is not sufficient for the Headmastership of a
Sccondary or Intermediate School. How can parents
be reasonably expected to send their children to such
an out-of-the-way place as Ring, and to maintain them
there at considerable expense for the purpose of being
educated by an ex-Second Class National School
Teacher, while National Teachers of qualifications
oqually as high, if not higher, are at their very doors?
To ask parents to do so seems to me to be preposterous
and absurd. 4

““Mapbdn’’ states:—“T am so well satisfied with it
up to the present that if my children were boys, and
big enough, I should send them to it straight away.”
If you intended your son for the Church, ¢ Mapddn,”
would you send him to the Ring Secondary School to be
taught Latin and Greek? If you intended him for a
commercial life, would you send him there to be taught
shorthand, typewriting, book-keeping, practical mathe-
matics, etc.? Would you send him there to be taught

stand, in ordinary National Schools; or to be pre-
pared for any position in the Civil Service, or for any
professional career whatsoever? If you would do so,

_ “Madan,” it would be,” in my opinion, at the

risk of materially injuring the prospects of your child.
And should you do it, that ig nop reason ):vhy other
parents should be called on to make a similar sacrifice.
Some Gaelic Leaguers are in the habit of sneering at
the education given in National Schools.  They in-
variably write the word  National ”” in this connection
with inverted commas. Yet, when the first Secondary
School was established by the Gaelic League, an ex-
Second * Class National School Teacher has heen con-
sidered sufficiently qualified to teach it.

I complained that the school was “ without a ve-
sponsible head, without a permanent teacher, sub-
Jjected to no supervision, and conducted by a gentleman
who has no pecuniary interest in the success of the
ischool,” and the public can now judge, from the
answers that have been given, whether these state-
ments are correct or not.

In the prospectus of the school, no mention was made
of a “responsible head’’; it merely states that “the
Central Executive of the Gaelic League had decided to
establish a Secondary or Intermediate School at Ring
with Mr. P. O’Kiely, Gaelic League Organiser for the
Deise, in charge.”” A body such as the Central Execu-
tive of the Gaelic League, from its very nature, can
only exercise the functions of a governing Board, or
a Board for providing the necessary funds, and cer-
tainly cannot be considered a ‘“head.” ‘“Mapbdn,”
however, informs us that Dr. Sheehan of Maynooth is
“the virtual Founder of, and is in supreme charge of
the school,”” but he does not say that Dr. Sheehan
regards himself as “responsible head ”” in the ordinary
meaning in which the words are used in the educational
world. The head of a Secondary School makes himself
responsible for the success of the school, for the re-
ligious interests of the pupils committed to his charge;
he is supposed to exercise personal supervision over the
daily work of the school, to see that backward pupils
receive due attention, to receive parents and often to
help them to decide on the choice of a career for their
sons, etc. In this sense, the Ring Secondary School is
without a: responsible head, though well provided with
Governors and Governing Boards.

Two replies have been given to my statement that
ithe school is “without a permanent teacher.” One
is by Mr. Kiely, who states:—“It has a permanent
teacher—as, permanent as any other Secondary
Teacher ;’” the other is by “ Manvan,’’ who says :—“The
Gaelic League has given Mr. Kiely to the Ring School
for a year absolutely. If the school be a success, he
will be left, himself being agreeable, in all probability,
to it permanently.”” (The italics are mine). I submit

that ““Mapban’y ”’ account is the correct one, and,
therefore, Tay original statement that the school has
not a pernfanent teacher remains uncontroverted.
Another statemenlt of Mr. Kiely’s has not been cor-
roborated. He states that the number of pupils attend-
ing the school is from 25 to 30. fiondn Mac Coluim
puts the number at “ from a dozen to twenty youngsters
besides some adults.” I ask my readers particularly to
note the arrangements that have been made for the
supervision of this school. Fiondn Mac Coluim, We
are told, pays an occasional visit. I did not think
fiondn undertook to examino Secondary or Interme-
diate Schools, or to direct their studies. If so, Gaclic
Teaguo Organisers possess educational gifts which have
been denied to ordinary men. Woe are further informed
that “at Christmas, at Easter, and again in Summer,

' Dr. Sheehan will thoroughly examine the pupils, and

overhaul things generally.”  In a normal condition of
things, pupils are not at school at these times, and I

‘do not consider it wise that the re ular vacations

ould bo broken up for the purpose of this overhauling.
T do not agree with “Maepban’’ that Mr. Kiely has
6 same pecuniary interest in the success of the school
is organising work. If the school became a
in the morning, he would suffer no financial loss,

THE LEADER. 253

he has his position as Organiser to fall back on,.
whereas, if he lost the latter, he would, like everyone
else who loses a position, have to look out for another,
and as happens in nearly all such cases, he would not
probably get one as good.

I am very glad to learn from “Mapban,’’ for the:
sako of the children who are attending it, that the de-
fects which I pointed out in the school buildings, and
the want of teaching appliances, have been remedied.
I fully accept this statement that “the school has all
the necessary appliances for teaching, maps, pictures,
ete., ete. ; is provided with a stove; has its walls felt,
coated for greater warmth; is, in a word, now
well filited up. In fact, the bill for all the
expenses voted for the school by the Coiroe
Snota has this week been presented to that
body, the work of improvement, which was considered
necessary, having been completed.”’ (Again the italics
are mine). I could not summon up sufficient energy to
make, as “ Mapdan’’ has done, a “ tour in the Decies ”
on a bicycle at this season of the year, and, conse-
quently, have not seen the improvements that have
been made in the Ring School and completed last week.

I think Mr. Kiely has taken my criticisms too
seriously; we have not been accustomed to honest
criticism in Irish life. I believe Mr. Kiely had been
a very capable National School Teacher, and a very
successful Organiser; and I do not see that he has had
sufficient reason for losing his temper, as he evidently
has done, at the suggestion of his being not so well
suited for the duties of Head Teacher of a Secondary
or Intermediate School. .

One of the effects of the present Language Movement
is that some men, if they happen lto have a knowledge
of the spoken language, consider that that is in itself
sufficient qualification to pose as experts in teaching, in
music, and as authors, playwrights, etc.

It is refreshing to find a countroversialist like
“ Manbdn”’ generously attributing honest motives to
one holding different views from his own. I assure
““Mapban”’ that I have tried to put myself in the
position of the Founders of the Ring Secondary School,
and absolutely fail to see how a Secondary or Inter-
mediate School could hope to succeed at Ring. The
project seems to me to be a wild and impracticable one,
and calculated to do great harm to the Language Move-
ment. If a few more such schemes were put forward,
the people would soon come to look upon Gaelic
Leaguers as visionaries and faddists, and would be slow
to follow their lead. 3

My principal object in criticising the Ring Secondary
School was not “to help it-on,”” as “M4&pban " thinks,
but to help on the Language Movement, and this, “in
my opinion, can be best secured in Ring by ending the
“Ring Secondary School.” ~ Mr. Kiely supplies me
with an additional reason for believing this school can
do no good, that it is already doomed to failure, viz.,
that the P.P. is opposed to it, and has denounced
from the pulpit. But, perhaps, it is not a Secondary
School at all, and that the whole misunderstanding has
arisen from its name. “Mapbdn ™ in his reply to me
now calls it “ The Ring Irish School,” and Mr. Kiely
informs us that “there is no special patent for the
name Secondary School. The Founders of the Second-
ary School in Ring have their own ideas about Primary
and Secondary Education.”’ I should say they have, if
they style a school attended by from a dozen to twenty
youngsters ’ a Secondary School.

The School is a Secondary School or it is not. It
has been described in the Prospectus supplied to the
Press, published in Waterford News, November 2nd, as
a Secondary or Intermediate School. If it is not what i
purports to be, the responsible authorities should say so:
on a matter of thiskind there should be no equivocation.
Parents should not be asked to send their children to
this school on the understanding of its being a Second-
ary or Intermediate School, if only one language is
taught in it, or the very elements of education. -

As I have now attained my primary object, viz., to
draw public alttention to this school, T do not intend to
return to the subject again. Dpicn.

COALS OF FIRE.

T HERE was a crowd of them gathered outside the
chapel after the Hoty Family Meeting. Mus.
Kenny was describing a new dress she had making fox
“Palace Day.”

“Oh, ’tis gorgeous,” said she, rolling her eyes sky-
wards. “ Asd such doaty little blue buttons down the
body! And a lovely lace collar! It seems a shame
ever to wear it out of the common.”” She was from
Dublin, and her only respeotablo adjectives were
“lovely,”” “ gorgeous,” and “doaty,” with “ massive ’’
as a reserve. There were others that came into use—
vigorous use—when she went “on the batther.”” But
they were unprintable, and that only happened now and
again. At present she was a teetotaller of over four
months’ standing, and quite regular at tho Holy Family
Meetings. She was looking forward to the great
League-of-the-Cross day, at the Crystal Palace, with as
much zest and eagerness as the oldest Leaguer among
them.

““ Are you going, ma’am?”’ she asked, turning to Mrs.
Crowley, who stood silent a few feet away, looking
askance at Mrs. Murray, between whom and herself
there had been bad blood because of a few words they
had at the last St.-Patrick’s-Night Concert.

“Ts it me, ma’am?’ said Mrs. Crowley. “Did you
hear tell of a robbery at all? Sure, I haven’t a decent
stitch on me. I'm promising myself a day at the
Palace this ten year, but I never get beyond the
promise.’’

“Oh, you should go,’”” urged Mrs. Kenny. “’Twill
be a lovely day. And the march past, with the Cardi-
nal, like @ saint out of Heaven, pouring blessings down
on them, oh, ’tis—’tis gorgeous.”” Again her eyes
expressed ecstasy. ““Oh, I declare you showld go.”

Mrs. Crowley’s bonnet strings, which she never wore
fastened, hung down on her bosom. With a vague
idea of reverence, she arranged them so always in the
House of God. Outside, they were usually flung over
her shoulders. She flung them back now as she
answered lightly, but with a real passion of longing
underlying her words.

“Ah. no. My day of pleasuring is over. But I'd
like well to go. I was there years ago, when I had
Mary just out of long clothes; and a.lovelicr day, thank
God, I never remember. Yes, indeed, and well dressed
T was, too. But I’ll come out, please God, to hear the
band going off in the morning. It puts a little I'fe
into a person to hear the fine old Irish tunes they
play.” And, with a comprehensive “ Good night and
good luck!” she was off, fearful of hearing more of a
day’s pleasure that tormented her with unsatisfied
desire.

“ Musha, God help her!”” said kind Mrs. Kenny.
“T declare I'd like well to see her down there next
Monday. She’s a great slave to that bothered old
fool of a husband of hers. He does be dreaming and
smoking and going on out of him about politics the
half of his time, instead of mending people’s shoes.
Bedad, if I had him ——.’’ She paused eloquently,
and Mrs. Murray answered, a little sourly: “There’s
a pair of them, I think; a pair of angishores. As God
made them, He matched them. The divil a much good
they’ll ever do, God forgive me for saying so. They’ll
mind anyone’s business but their own.”

“ Well, now,”” urged Mrs, Kenny, ‘“she’s the kindest
creature in the world. I know thero’s a little some-
thing between you, but, I declare, if you were ill or in
want, to-morrow, she’d be the first -to do you a
neighbourly turn.”” Mrs. Murray granted, unbeliev-
ingly, but she said no more. ‘She promptly and com-
pletely forgot the whole matter, for “Palaco Days’
and pleasuring in general were things to which she was
not given.

On the next night, Thursday, it was brought back
again to her in unexpected fashion. Her best lodger,
a nice young Customs Officer, who had two rooms all
to himself, presented her with a ticket, for the much-
talked-of Féte, a ticket for rail and Palace combined.
Tmportunity brought him to buy it, but loud rumours

DECEMBER 8, 1906.

of the spacious delights of “Palace Day ” forbade its
use. For he was very superior, as well as nice, and he
felt that ten hours of the glitter of green-and-gold
sashes and banners, and of the never-ceasing music,
blown from pipe and fife, would be too much for him.
The ticket had a most disturbing effect on Mrs. Murray.
For awhile she half-meditated using it herself; then
she put the idea away resolutely. It would mean
wasting a whole day, and, perhaps, getting her @hmgs
ruined. For the crowd was sometimes very enthusiastic.
Yot sho did not like to waste it; to do so wouid shock
hor cconomic sense, which was really highly developed.
Yet sho was not all economic sense. There stole in
upon her, yes, even in on hard old Mrs. Murray, a
thought that, some poor creature might get a day’s hap-
piness out of it. Some poor creature! The term
brought Mrs. Crowley to her mind, and she frowned and,
shook her head. That person had a.great deal too much
old chat, for one thing, and no proper respect for
people who had got on in the world. Besides, did she
not say she had not a decent stitch on her? No, Mrs.
Crowley was clearly out of the running. Yet, although
she thought of many and many a “ poor creature "
B was full of them—none seemed so really deserv-
ing as Mrs. Crowley. By Saturday she had conquered
her resentment so far as to decide that Mrs. Crowley
should be the recipient, and, coming home from Mass on
Sunday morning, she actually determined that a gift of
a few suitable old clothes should go with the ticket. A
miracle, you say? You would say so if you knew tough
old Mrs. Murray, yet the miraculous happened. Who
knows what sort of woman she might have been if the
world had gone easier with her? It is not hard to
smile out on a world that smiles at you. If Mis.
Murray had forgotten how to smile and be gracious,
she might well plead that she had bad example and poor
encouragement when her character was in the making.
Hardship at home, and “long days of labour and nights
devoid of ease,”” for many years in exile, had certainly
wiped away any outer seeming of .charm or gentleness,
but somewhere within her there must have been a
hidden spring of sweetness. Any way, on this occasion
she did the right thing in quite the right way. She
had a pretty well-filled wardrobe. In these days of
opulence, her taste ran rather to beads, and stamped
velvet, and startling arrangements of colour.  She
picked out a skirt and cape that had not been worn for
many a day, two really gorgeous garments, but ones
which the envious ladies of B- had by now forg:tten.
These in a bundle she took down to the Convent in the
afternoon, and there she and Sister Mary Laurence
arranged their presentation to Mrs. Crowley with the
ticket. She knew the spirit of her old antagonist, and
could well imagine Mrs, Crowley putting the bundle
and the ticket out into the roadway if she knew from
whence they came. And her economic sense was
shocked at the thought of the ticket being refused
and wasted at the last moment. At least we’ll put it
that way.  But Sister Mary Laurence told Mother
Prioress that Mrs. Murray was Irish enough to have
fine dnstincts surviving after years of London hardship
and buffeting. Mother Prioress smiled assent, knowing
that in the good Sister herself pride of race was ram-
pant, though the home of her race was unknown to her.

That evening in the Convent the gifts were bestowed
on the dazed and delighted Mrs. Crowley. ¢ They are
from a gentlewoman, a friend of mine,’’ said the Sister
gravely, fingering the garments on which the recipient’s
ravished eyes were feasting. Mrs. Crowley had no doubt
of it. Who but a thorough lady could have worn a slirt
s0 beautifully flounced, a cape so richly beaded? She
hardly slept a wink that night, and next morning she
was in the very front rank of those who marched with
the Guavds behind the screaming fifes from B—— to
London Bridgo.
